Al had recently been driving bus for Lamer's part-time. He was an active member of the HAM radio organization, and enjoyed the camaraderie with his fellow radio co-workers. Al will be sadly missed, but his love and personality will live on in family and friends who knew and loved him.

Al leaves behind his wife: Nancy Johnson, son: Nathan Johnson and 2 grandsons: Mason and Grant. He was preceded in death by his parents, son: Andrew Johnson and an infant sister.

The funeral service for Al will be held on Friday, July 10, 2026, at 12:00 p.m. at the Fellowship Baptist Church, 1130 Field Road, Kronenwetter, WI 54455. Pastor Brent Moodie will officiate. Visitation will be held on Friday, July 10, 2026 from 10:00 a.m. until the time of the service, also at the church. The burial will take place on a later date at the Perry Lutheran Cemetery in Mount Horeb.

In lieu of flowers, please direct donations in Al's name to either the Fellowship Baptist Church or to the HAM radio club.

A special thank you to all of Al's friends, family, and his church family, especially, Pastor Brent Moodie, for all of their prayers, support and comfort that was shown to Al and Nancy during his final days.

records.name: Barbara Ann Jennison
records.funeral_home: Brainard's Funeral and Cremation Center, Wausau
records.summary: Barbara Ann Jennison, age 82, of Weston, passed away on July 2, 2026.
records.body:
Barbara Ann Jennison, Weston, WI, died the morning of July 2, 2026, from the effects of vascular dementia. Barb was born in her family home in Amherst, WI, on November 4, 1943, the daughter of Phillip Charles Hamilton and Blanche (McGinley) Hamilton. Her brother, Phillip, Jr., arrived in August 1945 and together they experienced the hospitalization of their mother six weeks later to recover from tuberculosis, the divorce of their parents soon after, and a move with their mother to Wisconsin Rapids, where they settled. There, Barb's Catholic faith was formed in childhood as she attended SS Peter and Paul Elementary and later the Our Lady Queen of Heaven School before graduating in 1963, alongside her brother, from Assumption High School.

After graduation, Barb worked at Emmons Stationary and moved to Minneapolis in 1964 to attend the Humboldt Institute, where she completed a course in Airline and Business Training in 1965. There, a lifelong passion for traveling began, strengthened during her first job at Northwest Orient Airlines as a reservation agent. Barb returned to central Wisconsin as a ticket agent for Northwest Orient in the Wausau Airport, and in August 1966 she married Lewis W. Perkins, moving to Schofield where they raised their three children, Scott William, Sarah Jean, and Shelley Lynn. They divorced in 1979.

At the American Automobile Association, Barb experienced the business side of the travel industry by managing the travel agency in their Wausau office. Travel became a career path that she would continue to follow throughout her life, and she began roaming across the United States and around the globe—passport in hand—to share reviews with her clients and better inform her sales technique. Souvenirs from Alaska, Hawaii, Germany, Ireland, and Mexico lined her office shelves. Barb met James L. Jennison through friends, and the pair married in November 1982. In Jim, she found a partner just as interested in the travel business, and when Barb ventured out on her own to open The Travel Agency (later Jennison Travel) in Rothschild, he joined as the part-time office manager.

Through her travel agency, Barb organized and escorted group tours, coordinated destination weddings, planned honeymoons, booked young families on their first vacations, and made many friends—most beginning as clients. She became a Certified Travel Agent, and later joined MAST, a Midwest consortium to educate, train, and support travel agents, earning many awards from them for her business successes and dedication to the field. With the change in the travel reservation business increasingly moving toward phone and internet reservations, Barb brought her business into her Schofield home in 2001, a year that further changed the travel industry. For over twenty years she grew Jennison Travel, eventually closing when her health, and Jim's, began to decline.

As much as she loved to travel, she perhaps more so enjoyed time at home with family. It wasn't uncommon for her and Jim to enjoy a glass of wine as they watched the sun set from their deck, and the grill was always ready to go for a family meal to celebrate life events and birthdays. Fridays were often spent waiting for a table at Carmelo's for fish fry, or at Peking or The Palms Supper Club, the latter a favorite for regular dinners with her friends. Always one to enjoy a party, she celebrated her 80th birthday in 2023, just as the effects of her dementia began surfacing, surrounded by cousins from the McGinley, Lemke, and Hamilton clans, close friends, and neighbors.

Barb was predeceased by her parents, Blanche Hamilton and Phillip Hamilton, Sr.; her stepmother Delores Hamilton; her husband, Jim Jennison; her brother Phillip Hamilton, Jr.; her step-son Jay Jennison; her granddaughter Ellianah Bolduc, and nephew Joe Hamilton.

Surviving her are son Scott Perkins, Pittsburgh, PA; daughters Sarah (Ken) Maciaz, Kronenwetter, and Shelley (Ryan) Wucherer, Green Bay; and stepdaughters Jami (Joe Mershon) Traska and Jill (Tim) Bolduc. Missing their "Gramma Babs" are her grandchildren Liz (Luke) Hilleshiem, Peri Maciaz, Madi Wucherer, Ben Wucherer, Duke Wucherer, Halle (Mathew) Axling, Courtney Bolduc, and Brooke Jennison. The youngest family members, her great-grandchildren, Vivienne, Ava, and Riley Hilleshiem, will hear stories of great-grandma Barb for years to come. Her sisters-in-law, Donna Landeen and Dorie Hamilton, niece Niki Hamilton, and nephew Phil Hamilton III, will miss catching up with her, as will lifelong friends Mary Towle and Sharon Lorenzen. Too many to name, Barb's cousins from the McGinley, Lemke, Wirtz, Ecker, Skibbie, and Buehning families will remember the sparkle in her eye, her laugh, strong family devotion, and polka dancing at their weddings.

In her last weeks, Barb was comforted by the caregiving team from St. Croix Hospice, Wausau. Her family wishes to extend their gratitude to Becky, Brenda, and Jess at St. Croix as well as the entire team at Careview Transitional Care (formerly Pride TLC), Weston, for providing long-term assisted living care.

Visitation will be on Friday, July 31, 2026, at 9:30am at St. Anne Catholic Parish, Wausau, followed by a 10:30am funeral service led by Fr. Al Slowiak.

In lieu of flowers, the family asks you toast to Barb's life on your next trip—she would have loved to hear about where you stayed, what you ate, and all of the interesting sites you experienced while away. The many "Having a great time! Wish you were here!" postcards she received over the years speaks to her place in all of our hearts.

records.name: Claire A. Eggebrecht
records.summary: Claire A. Eggebrecht, age 67, of Pittsville passed away on Jul 8, 2026.
records.body:
Claire A. Eggebrecht was called home Wednesday, July 8, 2026, at Marshfield Medical Center, Claire was surrounded by her loved ones during her final hours.

Claire was born on November 23, 1958 in Marshfield, Wisconsin to James and Mary Lou Eggebrecht.

Claire enjoyed fishing and cooking, (including her famous chip dip). She especially loved spending time with her best friend enjoying music festivals, food shows and times out with the, side by side. She will be remembered for, her kindness, generosity, and her big heart. Above all else she especially cherished spending time with her grandchildren.

She is survived by her loving children, James (Stacey) Rollins of Marshfield and Mary (Travis) Rollins of Withee; grandchildren, Isaac and Chloe; lifelong loving friend, Lori Todd; aunt, Diane Kleba of Marshfield; sisters-in-law, Pam (Rick) Rollins of Chili and Lynn Eggebrecht of Minocqua; brothers, Jerry (Tari) Eggebrecht of Lake Havasu, AZ, Terry (Peggy) Eggebrecht of Minocqua, and Lea (Rachel) Eggebrecht of Abbotsford; and sister, Karen (Dean) Reinfleisch of Stratford.

Claire was preceded in death by her parents, James and Mary Lou Eggebrecht, and her brother, Brian Eggebrecht.

The family would like to extend their heartfelt gratitude to the doctors, nurses, and staff at Marshfield Medical Center for the compassionate care they provided to Claire during her final days.

A celebration of life to be announced at a later date.

Condolences may be sent online at www.rembsfh.com

records.name: Dale W. Hanne
records.funeral_home: Ascend Funeral Home & Cremation Care of Weston
records.summary: Dale W. Hanne, age 75, of Gilman, passed away on June 18, 2026.
records.body:
It is with great sadness that we announce the passing of Dale W. Hanne, 75 of Gilman, Wisconsin. He passed away peacefully Thursday, June 18th, 2026, at Aspirus Wausau Palliative Care with his daughter, Sheila, at his side, after losing a courageous health battle.

Dale was born January 23rd, 1951, to Elroy and Cecelia (Kramer) Hanne Sr. in Wood County, Wisconsin. He attended Colby Schools and became a truck driver immediately after, making it his lifelong career of over 45 years. He drove all over the continental United States loving every mile. He lived life to the fullest enjoying classic old vehicles, hunting, fishing, and spending time with family, good friends, and neighbors. He was always the life of the party, telling jokes and laughing. He loved making venison, grilling out, watching old western movies, making sausage, and listening to country music and the oldies with "Surfin Bird" as one of his favorites. He will be missed by everyone who had the pleasure of knowing him.

He leaves behind his loving wife and soulmate, Lynette, Daughters; Sheila (Jerry) Hauck, Heather (Kurt) Kulas, Jackie (Joel) Oechler and Hillary (Adam) Williams. Sons; Jeremy Heiden and Ricky Sazama. Grandchildren; Tyler, Kyle, Jaxson, Noah, Aaron, Hunter, Evelyn, and Bethany.

He was preceded in death by his parents, grandparents, daughter; Kristie and brother; Elory Jr.

A weekend of celebrating his life was held by his family per Dale's request.

records.name: David E. Raymond
records.summary: David E. Raymond, age 88, of Weston, passed away on July 5, 2026.
records.body:
David E. Raymond, 88, Weston passed away peacefully on Sunday, July 5, 2026 at his home.

He was born October 7, 1937 in Wausau, son of the late Harold and Angeline (Scutkowski) Raymond. On June 13, 1970 he married Connie Haight at Holy Name Catholic Church, Wausau. She survives.

For many years Dave was an electronic technician at AT&T. Some of his favorite pastimes included being outside, fishing, cutting wood and traveling. Dave had a passion for bear hunting and would be known to spend over a month in the bear woods alongside Connie.

Dave proudly served his country in the United States Air Force.

Survivors include his wife, Connie Raymond, Weston, one sister, Rosemary Wianecki, Eagle River and nieces and nephews.

At Dave's request there will be no formal services. A private service will be held at Fellowship Baptist Church, Kronenwetter at a later date.

records.name: Francis "Bud" Edward Fischer
records.summary: Francis "Bud" Edward Fischer, age 88, of Antigo passed away on July 8, 2026.
records.body:
Francis Edward "Bud" Fischer, 88, of Antigo, Wisconsin, passed away at home on July 8, 2026.

Bud was born on June 21, 1938, in Antigo, Wisconsin, to Frank Fischer Jr. and Marie Fischer (Smith).

He married his wife Joyce (Wickey) on November 14, 1959, in Milwaukee. Together they built a life and a family that reflected everything he valued.

Bud served his country in the United States Marine Corps, where he worked as a truck mechanic. After his service, he went to work for Bultman Trucking in Milwaukee. He later spent ten years at Consolidated Freightways in Wausau before returning to Bultman Trucking, where he remained until he retired after thirty years of driving with the Teamsters.

Anyone who knew Bud knew that his work didn’t stop when he retired. He was a man of many talents — a self-taught carpenter, electrician, mechanic and welder who could build or repair just about anything. He never hesitated to lend a hand to anyone who needed it, and he took pride in passing on those skills and hard-working attitude to his children.

Bud was happiest outdoors. He loved to hunt and fish, work on his tractors, and he was most at home in the woods. You could always find him in his shop building and fabricating just about anything. He spent many summers at the family cottage on Oxbow Lake in Presque Isle, Wisconsin, and loved camping in Michigan’s Upper Peninsula near Lake Superior. And wherever Bud went, you could always find him with a can of Skoal in his chest pocket.

He is survived by his wife Joyce; his daughters Yvonne (Scott) Nutter and Dianne (Mike) Kohler; his son Jerry (Wendy) Fischer; his sister Shirley Laab; four grandchildren; and four great-grandchildren.

He was preceded in death by his parents, Frank Fischer Jr. and Marie Fischer; his sister Joann Gehrke; and his great-grandson Loxley.

A celebration of life will be held in the fall. Details to follow. Mid Wisconsin Cremation Society is assisting the family. Online condolences and words of remembrance may be left at MWCS.WS.

records.name: Gilbert Edward Slivicke
records.summary: Gilbert Edward Slivicke, age 88, of Hatley passed away on Jul 8, 2026.
records.body:
Gilbert E. Slivicke, 88, Town of Reid, passed away on Wednesday, July 8th, 2026, at home, with his wife Bonnie of 60 years at his side.

Gilbert was born November 11, 1937, on the family farm, son of the late, Thomas Sr and Julia (Chmiel) Slivicke, where he was raised and developed the strong work ethic and values that would guide him throughout his life. He attended Rosenberry grade school in a one-room schoolhouse before going on to Mosinee High School. After school, he and his brother Tom, moved to Milwaukee in search of work. After trying many different professions, Gilbert discovered his passion for the concrete industry. Through hard work, determination, and integrity, he built a successful business that he proudly owned and operated as Slivicke & Sons Cement Contractors. Retiring at 73.

In 1958, Gilbert married Laverne Paszkiewicz, daughter of the late Anthony and Marion (Golembiewski) Paszkiewicz. Later, in 1966, after Laverne’s passing, he met and married Bonita Geiger-Fobes, who was also widowed. She was the daughter of the late Joseph and Katherine (Pupp) Geiger. Together, they built a beautiful, blended family, embracing children from each of their previous marriages and welcoming one child they had together. Their 60 years together were a testament to enduring love, unwavering commitment, and the family they created.

Gilbert loved hunting and fishing with family and friends. His passion for hunting took him across the United States and on the adventure of a life time to Africa. Gilbert was known for his dedication, generosity, and the lasting relationships he built throughout his life.

Gilbert is survived by his wife, Bonnie Slivicke; his sister Joyce Oertel-Gantner, his children, Michael (Linda) Slivicke, James (Joanne) Slivicke, Mary (Marvin) Schultz, and Scott (Kim) Slivicke; nine grandchildren, Sylvia (Rick) Green, Lorna (Steven) Richter, Jonathon (Megan) Slivicke, Joshua Slivicke, Jessica Slivicke, Marvin Schultz, Lucas (Brittany) Slivicke, Jacklyn (Phil) Scott, and Ashley (Brad) O’Connell, and thirteen great grandchildren.

Besides their parents, Gilbert was preceded in death by, his first wife, Laverne Slivicke; brother Thomas Slivicke Jr, son Bill Fobes-Slivicke, brother in-laws Charles (Chuck) Oertel, Claude Gantner, Jerry Behnke, Daniel Fluegge, Gerald Geiger, and Donald Schmidt, brothers and sisters in-law Daniel & Gloria Kleman, and Dennis & Stephanie Geiger.

A Funeral Service will take place at 12 noon, on Tuesday, July 28, 2026, at Beste Funeral Home, Mosinee. Father Aaron Becker will officiate. Visitation will be from 10:00 a.m., until the time of the service, all at the Funeral Home. Online condolences and memories may be directed to the family at www.bestefh.com.

records.name: John "Jack" E. Bobinski
records.summary: John "Jack" E. Bobinski, age 85, of Rothschild passed away on Jul 8, 2026.
records.body:
John E. Bobinski (Jack), 85, of Rothschild, passed away peacefully on Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at home surrounded by the love of his family and dear friends. He leaves behind a legacy of faith, service, compassion and unwavering love for his family.

A dedicated pediatrician of more than 30 years, he devoted his professional life to caring for children and supporting families with kindness, wisdom, and genuine compassion. To the countless patients whose lives he touched, he was more than a physician–he was a trusted source of comfort, hope and healing.

Jack was born on January 12, 1941 in New Haven, CT to Margaret Bobinski. He graduated from Hamden Hall and attended Fairfield University in CT. During his time in high school and college, Jack worked at a YMCA camp for children with disabilities as a counselor and, eventually, camp director. It was at Camp Hubinger that he was determined to spend his life paying special attention to children with special needs. From Fairfield, Jack moved to medical school at St. Louis University where a chance meeting turned his life in an entirely new direction. On October 15, 1964, the St. Louis Cardinals beat the New York Yankees to win the World Series, and word quickly spread around the city that a giant party was being thrown with free beer from Budweiser. At Gaslight Square in downtown St. Louis, Jack did not find any free beer, but he did meet a beautiful blonde named Teresa Nicholas and immediately fell in love. They married on September 4, 1965. After graduating from medical school, Jack began his internship at Cardinal Glennon Children’s Hospital in St. Louis. He was drafted into the U.S. Air Force in 1969 and spent two years at the air base in Duluth, MN where he was discharged as a major in 1971. He returned to Cardinal Glennon becoming Chief Resident in pediatrics. From there, Jack moved his family to Wausau, WI to become the second pediatrician in the city at the Wausau Medical Center. He was instrumental in the Neurodevelopment Clinic, helping children with special needs and starting Rainbow’s End Day Camp. He served on the DC Everest School Board from 1974-1977. Jack retired from practicing medicine in 2020 but never stopped caring for others in any and all ways possible.

Jack cherished his family more than anything. Jack was a devoted husband, loving father, proud “Poppy”, and loyal friend whose greatest joy came from simply being with the people he loved. Whether sharing a laugh, showing up for people when they needed it most, or offering a quiet word of wisdom, he made those around him feel valued and deeply loved. Summers spent at the family cottage fishing, boating and laughing were what made Jack the happiest. He loved giving gifts to everyone and always thought of others in ways that made them feel so special. Christmas mornings packed into the small family room were filled with laughter and love and always with special surprise gifts from Poppy. He was an avid reader and had an extensive library at home that he shared with people he loved. His love for sports was shown in his enthusiastic support for all things Packers, Brewers, Badgers, Missouri Tigers and Texas A&M Aggies. And you would always find him at Steihm Stadium on a Friday night cheering on his beloved Evergreens.

Jack was a faithful Catholic, and it shaped every part of his life. It guided the way he loved, served, forgave and lived each day. His quiet devotion and steadfast trust in God were an example to all who knew him.

The traditions he created, the love he gave, and the values he instilled in his children and grandchildren will forever be his greatest legacy. He lived a life marked by service–to his country, his patients, his church, and above all, his family. His example of compassion, humility and unconditional love will continue to inspire all who had the privilege of knowing him. Jack will be remembered for his kind heart, deep faith, quiet strength and infinite wisdom.

None of his accomplishments would have been possible had Jack’s life taken a different turn. He will forever be grateful to his friends and colleagues at his Thursday morning AA meeting who helped Jack stay sober since 1983.

Jack is survived by his loving wife of almost 61 years, Teresa. He is survived by his children Ann (Terry) Geier, John (Abbey) Bobinski, and Lisa (Cullen) Sloan. He is also survived by the pride and joys of his life: his nine grandchildren Whitney (Jackson), Sydney (Isaac), Olivia, Trey, Jack, Nicholas, Ava, Ian and Grace. He was preceded in death by his mom, Margaret.

The family would like to thank the love and care provided by Cornerstone Caregiving especially Andrea and Alecia who became like family to Jack in the past year. We also thank Jack’s close friends Jimmy Lombardo, Bob Poole, Darryl Witt, Tom Helbach and Red Courtmanche for their unwavering love and friendship. Our sincere gratitude to Jason Schulta and the staff at Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home for taking care of Jack at the end. Jack would have wanted everyone to know that he was at Jason’s birth as his pediatrician and how full circle it was to have Jason take care of him at the end of life.

The Funeral Mass will be celebrated at 11:00 a.m. Friday, July 17, 2026 at St. Mark Catholic Church, Rothschild. The Rev. Janusz Kowalski will preside. Burial will be in Gate of Heaven Cemetery, Kronenwetter. Visitation will be held on Thursday, July 16, 2026 from 4:00 p.m. until 7:00 p.m. at the Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home, 1302 6th Street, Wausau and again on Friday from 9:00 a.m. until the time of services at the church. In lieu of flowers a memorial fund has been established in Jack’s name. Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home, Wausau is in charge of arrangements. Online condolences may be expressed at www.petersonkraemer.com

records.name: Kelly Melanson
records.summary: Kelly Melanson, age 59, of Kronenwetter, passed away on July 4, 2026.
records.body:
Kelly Melanson, age 59, of Kronenwetter, Wisconsin, passed away in the arms of her husband on Saturday, July 4, 2026, at her residence after a two-year battle with cancer.

Born on July 11, 1966, in Wausau, Wisconsin, Kelly was the daughter of Sandra Halverson. Kelly's journey was marked by warmth, laughter, and a deep devotion to those she cherished.

Throughout her years, Kelly embodied the very essence of kindness and generosity. The world knew her as loving, caring, and outgoing.

Family stood at the core of Kelly's life. She shared a beautiful marriage with her husband, Roy, whose steadfast companionship was evident to all who knew them. Together, Kelly and Roy found joy in the simplicity of working on their property, moments filled with nature, laughter, and the quiet satisfaction of shared achievements.

Work was not merely a job for Kelly but an extension of her caring spirit. As a dedicated bus operator for Metro Ride with the City of Wausau, she served her community with diligence and heart, touching the lives of countless riders over the years. Her professionalism and conversation were daily gifts to those who crossed her path.

Kelly found her true joy in the great outdoors. Whether she was fishing, hunting, or simply enjoying time in nature, she felt most alive with wind in her hair and soil beneath her boots. She and Roy's shared love for their land was a source of happiness, adventure, and connection. Kelly's fond membership in Bill's Musky Club, where she was recognized as a lifetime member, stands testament to her enduring passion for angling and the friendships that grew from her love of nature.

Being a grandmother was among Kelly's greatest joys. Her granddaughters, Amelia and Elizabeth Kroeplin, lit up her life. She cherished every moment spent with them, creating loving memories in which they will forever find comfort. Kelly's adoration for her granddaughters was rivaled only by their admiration and affection for her.

Kelly is survived by her husband Roy; son, Kenneth (Kou Yang) Kroeplin; brother, Charles Kroeplin; and sister, Tamie (Joe) Williams. She is also survived by her sister-in-law, Brenda (Cary) Pellowski; granddaughters, Amelia and Elizabeth Kroeplin; uncles, Roger Frost and Richard Frost; many nieces, nephews, other relatives and many friends. She is preceded in death by her mother, Sandra Halverson.

A funeral service for Kelly will be held on Friday, July 10, 2026, at 11:00 AM, at Ascend Funeral Home & Cremation Care, 2809 Jelinek Avenue, Weston. Visitation will take place from 9:00 AM until the time of service at 11:00 AM. Kelly will be laid to rest following the service at Stettin Christian Cemetery.

As we gather to celebrate the life of Kelly, we invite those who wish to honor her memory by wearing camouflage attire.

records.name: Leona D. Carr
records.summary: Leona D. Carr, age 97, of Wausau, formerly of Marathon, passed away on July 4, 2026.
records.body:
Leona D. Carr, 97, of Wausau, formerly of Marathon, passed away peacefully on Saturday, July 4, 2026 at Mount View Care Center in Wausau, under the care of St. Croix Hospice.

She was born November 5, 1928 in Mosinee, daughter of the late Albert and Stella (Blaszak) Zawislan. Leona graduated from Mosinee High School in 1947.

Leona had a fulfilling and diverse work history which she often talked about. Her earliest job included housecleaning in "several beautiful homes on Wausau's East side". She was part of the 'Rosie the Riveter' era when she welded for Muskegon Piston in Schofield. She owned Carr's Bar in Town of Stettin. Leona moved on to what would be her primary career until retirement of being a CNA at Sunny Vale and Mount View Care Center. She also managed and bartended for American Legion, VFW, and Thunderbird bars in Wausau and worked in ginseng gardens. She spent a year living in Green Bay where she was the manager of a Hallmark Store. Leona had stories and enjoyed aspects of every job.

After retirement she was able to enjoy hobbies and her family found out how creative and crafty she was. She had always been great at cooking and sewing, but when she gardened, painted the most beautiful ceramics, and made some grandchildren baptismal gowns, it was amazing to see someone who spent their life often working two jobs at a time get to enjoy and express herself. Leona had an incredible work ethic and instilled that in her children.

Leona is survived by her children, Carol, Diane (Bob), Gary, Randy (Cheri), Brian (Judy), Lynn (Lynn) and her daughter-in-law, Char. Her grandchildren, Kim, Kris, Brian, Michael, Amy, Carrie, Christy, Casey, Dana, Gina, Kevin, Kelly, Kory, Kyle, Kody, Garret, Allison, Lucas, and Samantha. Leona has many more cherished great and great-great grandchildren. Another passion was to boast about all of her grandchildren's accomplishments or "how smart they were" when they were little.

She was preceded in death by her parents and siblings, John, Marie, Angeline, Ted, Sophie, Ben, Helen, Edward, Clara, Stella, and Bernice. Three sons, Wayne, Dale, and Patrick. A daughter-in-law, Doreen, son-in-law, Len, and granddaughter, Alicia also preceded her in death.

In accordance with Leona's wishes, there will be a private service and burial at St. Mary's Cemetery in Marathon.

records.name: Mary Kaehne
records.funeral_home: Ascend Funeral Home & Cremation Care of Weston
records.summary: Mary Kaehne, age 66, of Weston, passed away on June 29, 2026.
records.body:
Mary Kaehne, age 66, of Weston, Wisconsin, passed away unexpectedly but peacefully at her home on Monday, June 29, 2026, with her companion, Bella, her German Shepard, by her side.

Born on February 27, 1960, in the vibrant city of Chicago, Illinois, Mary's journey was marked by unwavering compassion, an ardent love of learning, and a steadfast dedication to those she held dear.

Mary distinguished herself academically, earning double bachelors, an achievement emblematic of her intellectual curiosity and relentless pursuit of knowledge. Her educational path laid a foundation for a vocation that would become not just her profession, but truly, her calling.

As a cherished nurse with Aspirus, Mary embodied the essence of care and advocacy. Her devotion to the well-being of her patients transcended duty; she brought a gentle, healing touch into every interaction, and her colleagues and patients alike felt buoyed by her compassion and unwavering commitment. To Mary, nursing was as much an art as it was a science a way to bridge empathy with expertise, and the community she served will long remember her for this rare gift.

Mary's passions extended far beyond the walls of her workplace. She was an avid reader, deeply engaged in holistic medicine, exploring the benefits of essential oils, and continually seeking knowledge that could enrich both her life and the lives of others. Her love for animals was evident in the affection she poured onto her beloved dogs and cats, whose companionship brought her great joy. Bird watching became a source of serenity and wonder, each encounter with nature nurturing her spirit. She also had a lifelong appreciation for jewelry, delighting in the beauty and craftsmanship of each unique piece.

Mary is survived by her beloved daughter, Starr Kaehne, and her treasured granddaughter, Emily Kaehne, both of whom were the lights of her life. She is also survived by her siblings; Nancy, Sue, Marc, Ron, and Bill, and her good friend Edward. She is preceded in death by her mother.

Per Mary's wishes, no formal services will be held.

records.name: Quentin William Milz
records.summary: Quentin William Milz, age 88, of Rhinelander passed away on July 8, 2026.
records.body:
Quentin W Milz, age 88, of Rhinelander, died on July 8, 2026 in Laona, Wisconsin, He was born on March 28, 1938 in Sheboygan, Wisconsin, the son of William and Roma (Marold) Milz. Survivors include his wife Jean Milz, his children Monica (Thomas) Trudeau and Dr. Michael (Amy) Milz and his grandchildren Abigail, Emily, Marissa and Ava.

A complete notice will follow. You may leave your private condolences for the Milz family at www.carlsonfh.com. The Carlson Funeral Home (715-369-1414) is serving the Milz family.

records.name: Tony Lenard
records.summary: Tony Lenard, age 74, of Marathon, passed away on July 4, 2026.
records.body:
Tony Lenard, age 74, of Marathon, WI passed away at home, surrounded by his wife, son, and sister, along with a blanket of many prayer wishes from other family members and friends on July 4, 2026. Tony was born in Wausau, WI and raised on a dairy farm north of Edgar. He was the son of Regina and Phil Lenard. Tony married Mary Wilichowski on Dec. 18, 1976.

Tony was a successful building salesman, owning Advantage Building Systems. He sold over 3,000 buildings throughout his more than 30 year career. He took pride in his projects and made a lasting impression of honesty and integrity in working with his customers.

Tony had a passion for motorcycles, whether they were complete bikes or various parts in a box. He loved cycle racing and the thrill of the finish line. In the area, Tony was known as a "legend in the field of biking." Tony was community-minded, being involved in projects at St. Mary's, Marathon High School, and Town of Stettin. He was a gifted craftsman, inventor, and problem solver. If someone had a question, Tony had the answer. Tony's son, Ryan, brought an immeasurable joy to his life. Tony never missed supporting Ryan with advice or sharing his 100% presence at music, academic, or sports events.

Survivors include his wife, Mary, and son, Ryan (Katie.) Tony's grandson, AJ (Anthony) was named after his grandpa. He delighted Tony as only a 3 ½ year old child can.

Besides Tony's parents, Tony was preceded in death by one sister, Margaret Treu.

A Celebration of Life will be held at 11:00 a.m. on Saturday, August 29, 2026 at Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home, 3400 Stewart Ave., Wausau. Rev. Msgr. Joe Diermeier will lead the service. Visitation will be from 9:00 a.m. until the time of service all at the Stewart Avenue funeral home. Private burial will be at St. Mary's Cemetery.

Any memorials may be directed towards the Edgar/Marathon Circle of Joy.

records.name: William "Bill" Clark
records.summary: William "Bill" Clark, age 92 passed away on July 8, 2026.
records.body:
William “Bill” Clark, born on January 15, 1934, to the late Thomas and Frances (Pryga) Clark, passed away surrounded by the love of his family on July 8, 2026.

Bill married the love of his life, Phyllis Krueger, and together they shared 57 wonderful years of marriage before Phyllis' passing in 2016. They were truly the perfect pair. Phyllis was the one who took charge, while Bill was content to go with the flow—a balance that served them well throughout their life together.

A quiet, easygoing man, Bill found joy in life's simple pleasures. He enjoyed ice fishing, hunting, gardening, playing cards, going out to eat, and, above all, spending time with his family and friends. His gentle nature and steady presence will be fondly remembered by all who knew him.

Bill worked for many years at the paper mill in Mosinee, Wisconsin, and proudly served his country in the United States Army, followed by the National Guard.

He will be dearly missed by his daughters, Betty (Mark) Martino and Judy (Dennis) Tiry; and his beloved granddaughter, Melissa (Andrew) Bella.

Bill was preceded in death by his parents, Thomas and Frances Clark; his beloved wife, Phyllis; and his daughter, Eydie Clark.

Private graveside services will be held at Gate of Heaven Cemetery, with military honors conducted by Montgomery-Plant-Dudley American Legion Post 10.

The family extends their heartfelt gratitude to the staff of Mount View and Aspirus Comfort Care & Hospice Services for the exceptional care, kindness, and compassion they provided to Bill.

John J. Buettgen Funeral Home is assisting the family with arrangements. Online condolences and words of remembrance may be shared at honorone.com.

records.name: Angeline "Angie" R. Kieffer
records.summary: Angeline "Angie" R. Kieffer, age 86, of Auburndale passed away on Jul 9, 2026.
records.body:
Angeline “Angie” R. Kieffer, 86, Auburndale, passed away on Thursday, July 9, 2026 at North Shore Healthcare at Marshfield.

A Mass of Christian Burial will be held at 12:00 pm on Wednesday, July 15, 2026 at St. Mary’s Catholic Church, Auburndale with a rosary service at 8:30 am followed by visitation until the time of service. Father Angi will officiate. A livestream of the service will be available on Angeline’s obituary page at the time of service. Burial will follow in the parish cemetery, and pallbearers will be grandsons, Troy Kieffer, Peter Bell, Kalvin Kieffer, Wade Kieffer, Neil Kieffer, Dustin Kieffer, Ethan Kieffer and Isaac Aschenbrenner.

Angie was born on February 17, 1940 in Auburndale to Walter and Theda (Carroll) Hamus. On January 14, 1961, she was united in marriage to Gordon “Gordie” Kieffer at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Auburndale. He passed away on January 19, 2020.

Angie was a devoted member of St. Mary's Catholic Church, where she sang in the choir, served as a Eucharistic minister, and participated in the liturgy readings. She enjoyed taking drives through the countryside in search of deer and turkeys, playing bingo, and celebrating family and friends by calling to sing "Happy Birthday" on their special day.

She is survived by 11 children, Bernard (Cindy) Kieffer, Arnold (Julie) Kieffer, Diann (Harold) Bell, Leroy (Laura) Kieffer, Dennis (Sarah) Kieffer, Jerry Kieffer, Donna (Mike) Heil, Russell (Cindy) Kieffer, Darnell (Kurt) Ziegler, Glen (Trisha) Kieffer, and Dori Aschenbrenner (Brian), daughter in law, Laurie Kieffer, 39 grandchildren, and 35 great grandchildren.

She is further survived by her siblings, Clifford (Ruthie) Hamus, Renee (Rodney) Bernitt, Terry (Debra) Hamus, and Roy Hamus, along with numerous brothers and sisters in law.

She was preceded in death by her parents, husband, sons, Dale Kieffer and Raymond Kieffer, grandsons, Adam Kieffer and Jeramie Eveland, sister, Gail Krueger, and daughter in law, Rhonda Kieffer.

The family would like to thank North Shore and Serenity for their excellent care.

In lieu of flowers, Memorial Masses can be offered to St. Mary’s Catholic Church in Angeline’s name.

records.name: Paulette A. Patzer
records.summary: Paulette A. Patzer, age 79 passed away on Jul 9, 2026.
records.body:
In Loving Memory of Paulette Ann (Panfil) Patzer

“Her hands served, her heart loved, and her legacy will live on forever.”

Paulette Ann (Panfil) Patzer, 78, of Wisconsin Rapids, Wisconsin, passed away on July 9, 2026. Born April 22, 1947, in Milwaukee County, Wisconsin, Paulette was the beloved daughter of Sigmund John Panfil and Florence (Iverson) Panfil.

Paulette was preceded in death by her fiercely beloved husband, Glendon Irvin Patzer; her father, Sigmund John Panfil, and her mother, Florence (Iverson) Panfil.

She is lovingly survived by her son, Sheldon Patzer, ex-daughter-in-law Connie Patzer; her daughter, Shannan Siekert, and son-in-law, Dennis Siekert; her grandchildren, Ryan Patzer, Bryanna Patzer, Leyton Patzer, Zackery Patzer and his wife, Cheyanne, Amanda Lynn Patzer, Ashton Patzer, Zade Patzer and his wife, Heidi, Kassie Schenck, Mackenzie Parr and her husband, Matthew, and Maverick Siekert; 17 cherished great-grandchildren; three siblings; and many extended family members, friends, former coworkers, and individuals whose lives were touched by her compassion and care.

Paulette was a woman defined by compassion, an unwavering work ethic, and a heart devoted to caring for others. Early in her career, she worked in the building now known as the U.S. Bank Center in Milwaukee, (was the First Wisconsin building) the tallest building in Wisconsin, and later worked at Sequoia Supply, in Wausau, Wisconsin. She eventually found her calling in healthcare, serving as a Certified Nursing Assistant at The Renaissance for nearly 20 years.

For Paulette, caring for others was never simply a job. She walked alongside people during some of the most challenging and vulnerable moments of their lives, offering dignity, comfort, and compassion when they needed it most. Her experience and wisdom extended beyond those entrusted to her care. She freely shared her knowledge and advice with the nurses and caregivers around her, helping shape stellar healthcare professionals who carried her lessons and compassion forward. Her influence lives on not only in the patients she cared for, but also in the caregivers she helped inspire.

Above all else, Paulette loved her family fiercely. The love she shared with her husband, Glendon, was equally fierce—a deep and enduring devotion that remained an extraordinary part of her life and the story of their family. Their love, commitment, and life together created a legacy that continues through their children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren.

Throughout her life, Paulette found joy in creating and nurturing beautiful things. In her earlier years, she enjoyed sewing and painting figurines. She also possessed a long-standing green thumb and found happiness working in her garden. Her love of gardening became a passion she passed on to her daughter, Shannan, and, in time, Paulette found herself learning even more from the daughter to whom she had first given that gift. It was a beautiful reflection of their relationship and the way Paulette’s lessons continued to grow through those she loved.

Those who knew Paulette will remember her compassionate spirit, her strength, and her incredible work ethic. She spent a lifetime caring for others—as a wife, mother, grandmother, great-grandmother, caregiver, mentor, and friend. She gave her time, her wisdom, and her heart freely.

The measure of Paulette’s life is found in the countless people she comforted, the caregivers she inspired, the family she fiercely loved, and the legacy she leaves behind. Her hands cared for many, but her heart always belonged to her family.

Though deeply missed, Paulette’s love will continue to live through every lesson she taught, every life she touched, and every memory her family carries forward. Her legacy will continue to bloom for generations, much like the gardens she so lovingly tended.

Family and friends are invited to gather in celebration of Paulette’s life on Thursday, July 16, 2026, at 4:00 p.m. at Memorial Park in Wausau, Wisconsin 54403. Online condolences may be expressed at www.petersonkraemer.com.

Please join Paulette’s family in celebrating her life. Guests are encouraged to bring a lawn chair, a memory, and a dish to pass, if they wish, as family and friends gather to share stories, remember, and celebrate a life so deeply loved.

Forever loved. Forever remembered. Her legacy lives on.

records.name: Bernard A. Kreuzer
records.summary: Bernard "Bernie" A. Kreuzer, 81, of Rib Mountain, passed away on July 3, 2026.
records.body:
Bernard "Bernie" A. Kreuzer, 81, of Rib Mountain, passed away on Friday, July 3, 2026, at Aspirus Hospice House. He was born in Milwaukee on July 2, 1945, to the late George and Lucille (Dietzler) Kreuzer. Bernie graduated in 1963 from Brookfield Central High School and later married Patricia "Patti" Waldinger on April 26, 1969, at St. Mark Catholic Church in Rothschild.

Bernie worked as a route salesman for Frito-Lay and retired after 28 years of calling on his many clients. After his retirement, he worked part-time for Brickner's and later for City Delivery. Bernie liked to stay busy and enjoyed feeding the birds, fishing, ice fishing, small game hunting, deer hunting, golfing, and bowling. Over the years he bowled in many leagues including Knights of Columbus men's and couple's with Patti. Bernie's different leagues also had him bowling at many of the bowling lanes (former and current) in the surrounding area like the Wausau Bowling Center, Mountain Lanes, Tanglewood Lanes, and Day's Bowl-A-Dome. He celebrated the day he rolled a 298 game. Bernie was a life member of the Knights of Columbus Council 1069 and a member of Holy Name of Jesus Catholic Church.

Dogs were a big part of Bernie and Patti's life. During Bernie's early hunting years, he had two Beagles that he hunted rabbits with. More recently they had the companionship of two Shih Tzus, Pixie and Molly.

Survivors include his wife, Patti; sister and Irish twin, Nancy (Don) Van Sleet; brother, John (Sue) Kreuzer of South Carolina; brothers-in-law and sisters-in-law, Elyse (Dave) Wiegand, Joanne Genrich, John (Chris) Waldinger, Robert "Bob" (Pat) Waldinger, and Mary (Jim) Scholes; and many nieces, nephews, and extended family.

He was preceded in death by his parents and brother, Jim Kreuzer.

The funeral Mass will be celebrated at 11:30 a.m. on Tuesday, July 21, 2026, at Holy Name Catholic Church, 1104 South 9th Avenue, Wausau, with Rev. Woodrow Pace presiding. Visitation will begin at 10:00 a.m. until the time of service, all at the church. Private burial will take place at a later date. Online condolences may be expressed at www.petersonkraemer.com.

The family requests memorial donations be made to Newman Catholic Classical School at Holy Name.

records.name: Celestine Tesch
records.funeral_home: Ascend Funeral Home & Cremation Care of Weston
records.summary: Celestine Tesch, also known as Tudy, age 78, of Wausau, Wisconsin, passed away on June 28, 2026.
records.body:
Celestine Tesch, also known as Tudy, age 78, of Wausau, Wisconsin, passed away on June 28, 2026.

Born on September 3, 1947, in Holcomb, Wisconsin. The daughter of Henry and Melba (Towle) Pahl. Celestine was, above all, a person devoted to her family. She cherished every moment spent with her children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren, holding each member of her family close to her heart and making them feel valued and loved in her special way. Those she cared for will remember her unfailing kindness, her curiosity, and her joy in both everyday moments and family milestones.

A gifted jewelry maker, Celestine had a creative spirit and a gentle hand. She found her passion in crafting beautiful, meaningful pieces, which she often gifted to loved ones. Her talents were a reflection of her character unique, thoughtful, and always shining with her warmth.

Tudy had a reputation as a good Samaritan and a genuinely good person. She was always willing to go out of her way to help others, embodying compassion in her actions and her words. She delighted in the simple pleasures of life, from running errands with zest earning her the affectionate title of "runner arounder" to browsing her favorite shops. Shopping was more than a pastime for Celestine; it was a source of connection and joy, an opportunity to think of others and perhaps find a small treasure to brighten someone's day. Those who knew Tudy will remember her most for her unwavering love, her giving nature, and her joyful presence.

She is survived by her grandchildren and great-grandchildren. Celestine is preceded in death by her parents, husband; Terry, and her children; Scott Tesch, Shelly Alsteen, Shawn Tesch and Sarah Janka.

records.name: Edward W. Ellefson
records.summary: Edward "Eddie" Winthrop Ellefson, 27, went home to be with the Lord on July 7, 2026.
records.body:
Edward "Eddie" Winthrop Ellefson
August 23, 1998 – July 7, 2026

Edward Winthrop Ellefson went home to be with the Lord on July 7, 2026, at the age of 27.

Eddie was a unique, complicated, passionate spark in the lives of everyone who knew him. He was kind-hearted and loving, creative and funny, witty and intelligent, a deep thinker with a talent and adventurous spirit all his own.

He loved music above almost anything. A gifted musician, Eddie taught himself to play a wide range of instruments, and he was precise and particular about the music he made, always chasing the sound he heard in his head. He was easy and warm in conversation, equally at home talking with someone twice or half his age. He was a visionary who saw things differently than most, and he left his mark on the people around him.

But his mind was never quiet. Eddie struggled with the debilitating grip of addiction on his quality of life, a battle that shaped so much of his story. His family is devastated by this loss. They prayed he would accept the help that was offered him, so he could find peace here on earth. That peace wasn't meant to be found in this life. He is now in the arms of Jesus.

Eddie is survived by his loving parents, Meg (Meyer) and John Ellefson, and his brother Davy, along with aunts, uncles, cousins, and many friends and acquaintances who loved him.

He was preceded in death by his beloved grandparents, John "Grandpa Jack" and Mary Meyer, and Richard and Barbara Ellefson; his great uncle Bill Field; and dear friends lost too soon.

Eddie was deeply loved by his mom, dad, and brother Davy, and by many friends and acquaintances whose lives he touched.

Memorial service will be 11:30 a.m. Saturday, July 18, 2026 at River City Church, Wausau. Visitation will be Saturday from 10:00 a.m. until the time of service all at River City Church. We invite all family and close friends to please attend.

Please dress in something bright and happy to honor Eddie's unique style.

records.name: James Robert Henke
records.summary: James Robert Henke, age 79 passed away on Jul 10, 2026.
records.body:
James Robert Henke, age 79, of Merrill, passed away on Friday, July 10, 2026, at his home.

He was born on July 8, 1947, in Wausau, son of Gilbert and Frances (Certano) Henke. He attended Copper River Schools from 1st to 8th grade and graduated from Merrill High School with the class of 1965. He enlisted in the U.S. Army, serving in Vietnam from 1966 - 1968. He was a workaholic up until he wasn’t able to work anymore. He was a superintendent at Boldt construction for over twenty years. He also owned his own logging business and the woods not only supplemented his living, but was also his hobby.

He was a very well respected, honest, hardworking man – a role model to many and was a great father and grandfather to his children and grandchildren. He also enjoyed playing billiards pool. On September 10, 1999, he married Marcia Howard, who survives.

A local businessman raised funds for a new sound system for the VFW to be used during Memorial Day, Veteran's Day, and Graveside services, as a Dedication to Sgt. Jim Henke. The sound system has a plaque dedicated to Jim that reads: "Honoring a silent hero whose voice will never fade."

He is survived by his wife, Marcia; his children, Shawn (Nicole) Henke of Merrill, Travis (Nancy) Henke of Wausau; grandchildren, Ben (Katie), Paige (Tanner), Zach, Jessica; two greatgrandchildren, Carter and Raelynn; stepchildren, Robin Schmidlkofer of Wausau, Renee Schmidlkofer of Merrill; three step grandchildren and 2 step great grandchildren; sister, Marge (Randy) Schultz; nephews, Jeff and Andy Boquist.

He is preceded in death by his parents, Gilbert and Frances.

The funeral service for James will be held on Thursday, July 16, 2026, at 11:00 a.m. at Taylor-Stine-Waid Funeral Home at 301 Eagle Drive, Merrill. Visitation will be held on Thursday, from 9:00 a.m. until the time of the service, also at the funeral home. Burial will be held in the Corning Methodist Cemetery off Greendale Road immediately following the service. The VFW Post 1638 will perform full Military Honors at the gravesite. Luncheon will follow the burial at the VFW Post 1638 in Merrill. In Lieu of Flowers, please make contributions to VFW Post 1638 in Merrill or St. Jude’s Children Research Hospital.

Taylor-Stine-Waid Funeral & Cremation Services of Merrill is assisting the family with arrangements. Online condolences are encouraged and may be made at www.taylorstinewaid.com .

records.name: John E. Bobinski
records.summary: John E. Bobinski (Jack), 85, of Rothschild, passed away peacefully on July 8, 2026.
records.body:
John E. Bobinski (Jack), 85, of Rothschild, passed away peacefully on Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at home surrounded by the love of his family and dear friends. He leaves behind a legacy of faith, service, compassion and unwavering love for his family.

A dedicated pediatrician of more than 30 years, he devoted his professional life to caring for children and supporting families with kindness, wisdom, and genuine compassion. To the countless patients whose lives he touched, he was more than a physician–he was a trusted source of comfort, hope and healing.

Jack was born on January 12, 1941 in New Haven, CT to Margaret Bobinski. He graduated from Hamden Hall and attended Fairfield University in CT. During his time in high school and college, Jack worked at a YMCA camp for children with disabilities as a counselor and, eventually, camp director. It was at Camp Hubinger that he was determined to spend his life paying special attention to children with special needs. From Fairfield, Jack moved to medical school at St. Louis University where a chance meeting turned his life in an entirely new direction. On October 15, 1964, the St. Louis Cardinals beat the New York Yankees to win the World Series, and word quickly spread around the city that a giant party was being thrown with free beer from Budweiser. At Gaslight Square in downtown St. Louis, Jack did not find any free beer, but he did meet a beautiful blonde named Teresa Nicholas and immediately fell in love. They married on September 4, 1965. After graduating from medical school, Jack began his internship at Cardinal Glennon Children's Hospital in St. Louis. He was drafted into the U.S. Air Force in 1969 and spent two years at the air base in Duluth, MN where he was discharged as a major in 1971. He returned to Cardinal Glennon becoming Chief Resident in pediatrics. From there, Jack moved his family to Wausau, WI to become the second pediatrician in the city at the Wausau Medical Center. He was instrumental in the Neurodevelopment Clinic, helping children with special needs and starting Rainbow's End Day Camp. He served on the DC Everest School Board from 1974-1977. Jack retired from practicing medicine in 2020 but never stopped caring for others in any and all ways possible.

Jack cherished his family more than anything. Jack was a devoted husband, loving father, proud "Poppy", and loyal friend whose greatest joy came from simply being with the people he loved. Whether sharing a laugh, showing up for people when they needed it most, or offering a quiet word of wisdom, he made those around him feel valued and deeply loved. Summers spent at the family cottage fishing, boating and laughing were what made Jack the happiest. He loved giving gifts to everyone and always thought of others in ways that made them feel so special. Christmas mornings packed into the small family room were filled with laughter and love and always with special surprise gifts from Poppy. He was an avid reader and had an extensive library at home that he shared with people he loved. His love for sports was shown in his enthusiastic support for all things Packers, Brewers, Badgers, Missouri Tigers and Texas A&M Aggies. And you would always find him at Steihm Stadium on a Friday night cheering on his beloved Evergreens.

Jack was a faithful Catholic, and it shaped every part of his life. It guided the way he loved, served, forgave and lived each day. His quiet devotion and steadfast trust in God were an example to all who knew him.

The traditions he created, the love he gave, and the values he instilled in his children and grandchildren will forever be his greatest legacy. He lived a life marked by service–to his country, his patients, his church, and above all, his family. His example of compassion, humility and unconditional love will continue to inspire all who had the privilege of knowing him. Jack will be remembered for his kind heart, deep faith, quiet strength and infinite wisdom.

None of his accomplishments would have been possible had Jack's life taken a different turn. He will forever be grateful to his friends and colleagues at his Thursday morning AA meeting who helped Jack stay sober since 1983.

Jack is survived by his loving wife of almost 61 years, Teresa. He is survived by his children Ann (Terry) Geier, John (Abbey) Bobinski, and Lisa (Cullen) Sloan. He is also survived by the pride and joys of his life: his nine grandchildren Whitney (Jackson), Sydney (Isaac), Olivia, Trey, Jack, Nicholas, Ava, Ian and Grace. He was preceded in death by his mom, Margaret.

The family would like to thank the love and care provided by Cornerstone Caregiving especially Andrea and Alecia who became like family to Jack in the past year. We also thank Jack's close friends Jimmy Lombardo, Bob Poole, Darryl Witt, Tom Helbach and Red Courtmanche for their unwavering love and friendship. Our sincere gratitude to Jason Schulta and the staff at Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home for taking care of Jack at the end. Jack would have wanted everyone to know that he was at Jason's birth as his pediatrician and how full circle it was to have Jason take care of him at the end of life.

The Funeral Mass will be celebrated at 11:00 a.m. Friday, July 17, 2026 at St. Mark Catholic Church, Rothschild. The Rev. Janusz Kowalski will preside. Burial will be in Gate of Heaven Cemetery, Kronenwetter. Visitation will be held on Thursday, July 16, 2026 from 4:00 p.m. until 7:00 p.m. at the Peterson/Kraemer Funeral Home, 1302 6th Street, Wausau and again on Friday from 9:00 a.m. until the time of services at the church. In lieu of flowers a memorial fund has been established in Jack's name.

records.name: Joseph E. Richter
records.summary: Joseph Edward Richter of Knife River, Minnesota, born September 12, 1948, went to be with Jesus on June 12, 2026.
records.body:
Joseph Edward Richter of Knife River, MN born September 12, 1948 to Joseph Francis Richter and Evelyn Ella (Wolfgram) Richter in Wausau, WI went to be with Jesus on June 12, 2026.

Joseph Edward Richter passed into the presence of his Lord and Savior after a life marked by being a man of the word of God, service for Him, joy, perseverance, love, and unwavering faith. He leaves behind an enduring legacy and powerful Christian testimony.

Joe was a man who believed life in Christ is directed by God's hand.

He grew up on the edge of Wausau, Wisconsin, on the wrong side of the tracks. His early years were marked by poverty, but because of his devoted family, there was always hot bread in the oven and a home-cooked meal when he arrived back from playing outdoors, often on the banks of the Wisconsin River, unsupervised, from sun-up to sun-down. Joe lived in a full home with his grandparents, parents, and six siblings. He never realized he was poor. He just didn't have the things other kids did. Joe always took along a little journal that he used to record the first hatch of dragonflies, which day the bluebirds began laying their eggs, what the fish were feeding on, and even where the fish species moved to on the river based on the weather.

One moment he revisited often was when he was only a young boy, around eight years old, he would ride his bike to some of his favorite wildlife areas, and along the way, he would pass by a church that had a sign out front. The sign read "You Must Be Born Again!" Because he didn't have understanding, he would cry and hope that one day he would be good enough to be born again.

Because of this defining moment where he was crushed by the impossible, misplaced, burden of earning salvation he devoted many of his later years to share the story of God's free gift by faith alone in his book titled "A Man After God's Own Heart". One lesson appeared again and again in those letters: a calling from God is not merely a destination; it is the journey itself. Joe believed that every season of life—whether in a laboratory, a classroom, a village, owning a bed and breakfast, or a family home—offered opportunities to serve God and love people.

One summer evening in 1965, a reluctant decision to attend a wedding dance changed everything. His friends surprised him with his first date! There, he met Pat, the young woman who would become his wife, partner in ministry, best friend, the great love of his life, and in the end years, his faithful caretaker. They danced the polka, the fox trot, and the waltz that first evening together, scarcely taking a single break. Joe knew that she was the perfect fit because she could keep up with him. Together, they would share a remarkable journey spanning decades of marriage, raising a family, scientific research, international service, missionary work, church planting, hardship, and joy.

As a young man, Joe pursued his passion for science and the natural world. His graduate research on the ecology of northern Minnesota lakes led to a career opportunity with the Environmental Protection Agency's Freshwater Research Laboratory in Duluth. There he joined a distinguished team of scientists conducting environmental research on the Great Lakes and freshwater ecosystems. His master's thesis was conducted on Shagawa Lake near Ely, MN.

Joe loved science. He loved discovery. He loved fishing. He loved hunting. He loved understanding how God's creation worked.

Yet he never viewed science and faith as opposing forces. To Joe, scientific inquiry only deepened his sense of wonder about the Creator.

While many might have chosen to keep their faith private in such an environment, Joe did the opposite. He openly shared his beliefs with coworkers, answered questions about Christianity, and eventually established lunchtime Bible studies among researchers and scientists. Those gatherings often met along the shores of Lake Superior.

His colleagues knew him as a gifted scientist. They also knew him as a man of conviction.

One of Joe's favorite EPA Lab stories involved a surprising intercom announcement at the laboratory: "Joe Richter, please come to the front desk. Jesus is here."

The resulting encounter with an eccentric, long haired, barefoot, traveler claiming divine authority, who ran out of gas near the lab, became one of many humorous stories Joe shared in his book to illustrate God's sense of humor.

Yet alongside those lighter moments were profound examples of compassion. When administrators became concerned about a troubled scientist struggling emotionally and mentally to the point of being clearly out of his mind, they turned to Joe for help.

Over lunch, Joe simply told the man, "I love you, and Jesus loves you." Those words opened the door to healing, friendship, and ultimately saving faith. Joe never claimed credit for what happened. He always pointed to the grace of God. He didn't give up on anyone, even those others had given up on.

Years before his missionary service began, Joe and Pat sensed a call from God to return to the Philippines and serve among the Igorot tribes of the northern mountains. They assumed the call would be immediate.

Instead, they waited ten years.

Looking back, Joe realized those years were not a delay but part of God's preparation. During that decade, he continued faithfully serving where God had placed him— At a tiny Lutheran church in Knife River, Minnesota. There, Joe and Pat faithfully led the youth, preached, and discipled.

Then, in 1983, after years of prayer, the call became clear.

Leaving behind a promising scientific career was not easy. Joe had recently been selected as a principal scientist on a 1.5 million dollar research project and had opportunities that many scientists spend their lives pursuing. Yet he believed God was leading him elsewhere.

With characteristic honesty, Joe later admitted that crippling fear of man nearly prevented him from taking that step. Even though he was an outspoken Christian, he worried what colleagues might think. He delayed submitting his resignation. The stress became so intense that he developed severe chest pain.

Only after finally handing in his resignation did the pain miraculously disappear.

The lesson remained with him for the rest of his life: fear of people can be powerful, but obedience brings peace.

To his surprise, his fellow scientists responded with admiration and encouragement. Some even contributed financially to support the mission work that lay ahead. Before leaving, Joe was invited to share with the whole laboratory his plans for mission service. After explaining fish farming and agricultural development in the Philippines, he concluded with what mattered most:

Improving lives was important, he said, but his greatest purpose was to share the love of God and the message of Jesus Christ. Without this; the rest is meaningless.

That commitment defined the remainder of his life.

Before mission service and working at the lab, Joe and Pat had first traveled to the Philippines as Peace Corps volunteers in the early 1970s. There, they worked in livelihood development, helping improve food production and economic opportunities in rural communities. During those years, they endured dangerous floods, life-threatening illness, political violence, severe culture shock, and significant hardship. When they first went, they didn't realize it, but they didn't love the people they were there to serve. They couldn't love them because they hadn't totally surrendered their lives to Jesus. Instead of experiencing the great high of scientific success and accomplishment of breeding Chinese Silver Carp, where it had never been done before, Joe fell into a deep depression.

He hated the poverty, the smell of urine, the lice, the pigpens attached to homes, the total lack of privacy, the snotty-nosed kids who always met them in the street to check what they had in their shopping bags, and the overwhelming filth in the village where they served.

Because of this great low, God was able to use the poorest family in the village and a bag of oranges to change their hearts. During Christmas of 1972, while living in the Philippines as Peace Corps volunteers, Joe and Pat totally surrendered their lives to Jesus Christ. This is the firm foundation upon which everything else was built.

Years later they returned as missionaries to the cordillera mountains of northern Luzon.

Their first day in Sagada became one of the defining stories of Joe's life. Barely settled into their new home, they accepted an invitation to preach in the remote mountain village. What they encountered that evening challenged them deeply. Joe often recalled the fear, uncertainty, and spiritual opposition they experienced in those early days. What happened that night included praying in the name of Jesus to set people free that were possessed by evil spirits.

Yet what remained with him most was not fear but faith.

He wrote often about how prayer carried him and Pat through situations beyond their own strength and how God's peace eventually replaced the fear that once threatened to overwhelm them.

The mountains that initially seemed intimidating became home for the next eight years.

There, they devoted themselves to serving the Igorot tribes. Joe, full of the power and love of God, fully loved the people of the mountains and considered it one of the greatest privileges of his life to live among them.

During the years of missionary service, Jabbok Church was planted. The name means a place of total surrender. Because of biblical, systematic, discipleship of the leaders the church thrives today. In fact, many new churches have been planted by the children of those first faithful believers. God's work continues today. It is true that the word of God will never return void.

Joe and Pat returned stateside in 1991, where they were asked to lead FARMS International.

Joe served faithfully as Executive Director for 25 years. FARMS International has programs in many locations around the world. The mission empowers Christian's to overcome poverty using God's principles. It is a radical model that doesn't leave the recipients dependent on Western money. God cares for the poor, and so did Joe. He was in so many ways A Man After God's Own Heart.

When God called him to build Superior Gateway Lodge in Two Harbors, Minnesota, it followed suit that it would be used for ministry. Joe often joked that he never had a more captive audience to share Jesus with. God sent many people there, unknowing, so they would hear the truth of Jesus Christ, freedom from the bondage of sin, and Salvation by faith. It was another chapter of an amazing adventure in Jesus for Joe and Pat.

Throughout every chapter of his story, Joe remained a teacher.

Whether writing letters to family members, preaching in churches, teaching Bible studies, mentoring young believers, or simply sharing a meal with a friend, he sought to encourage others to take one step of faith and see where it leads. His writings reveal a man who understood both hardship and hope. He never pretended life was easy. Instead, he pointed repeatedly to God's faithfulness in the midst of difficulty.

He often reminded his family that courage is not the absence of fear. Courage is trusting God enough to take the next step.

Joe's life was filled with those steps.

He was a scientist who saw God's fingerprints in creation.

He was a missionary who crossed many oceans to serve people he loved.

He was a storyteller who carefully preserved God's truths in story form for future generations.

He was a husband whose devotion to Pat never wavered.

He was a father, grandfather, and great grandfather who desired above all that his family would know and follow Jesus.

Most importantly, he was a man who sought to walk faithfully wherever God led him.

His life stands as a testimony. The lessons he shared through his letters continue to guide those who loved him. His example continues to inspire. His influence continues to bear fruit.

Joe was preceded in death by parents Joseph and Evelyn Richter, brother Donald Richter, Sister Jean Socha, brothers Gary Richter, David Richter, and daughter Calebanne.

Joe is survived by his beloved family, who carry forward the values he cherished and the faith he lived. He is survived by his faithful wife, Patricia Richter; his sisters, Dianne Bartig and Susan Haebig; his son, Luke Richter, and granddaughters, Faith (Andrew) and Hannah; his daughter, Belene Mclaughin, grandson Caleb, great-granddaughters Kaelyn and McKenna, grandson Nolan (Abberly), great-granddaughters Zayva and Vera, and grandson Darren; his daughter, Reseth (Jonathan) Oberg, and grandchildren Aaron, Titus, and Evemarie; and his daughter, Leah (Ronald) Bailey, and grandchildren Cherith, Joe, and Annie Joy.

As we celebrate his life, we remember one of the truths he often shared:

"Take one step and then see what God has for the next step. Never be in fear, for God is with you and will guide your steps."

Joseph Edward Richter spent his life taking those steps.

And by God's grace, he finished his journey very well.

Please join us for the celebration of his life.

Monday July 13, 2026
6:00 PM service followed by fellowship and meal
First Baptist Church Two Harbors, MN 55616

records.name: Martin E. Burkhardt
records.summary: Martin E. Burkhardt passed away, leaving behind a legacy of love, service, and lifelong learning.
records.body:
Martin E. Burkhardt passed away, leaving behind a legacy of love, service, and lifelong learning. He was born in Rock County, Wisconsin, to the late Martin and Georgina (Schneider) Burkhardt.

Martin met the love of his life, Kathleen Ihlenfeldt, and the two were married on January 28, 1961. Together they shared 65 wonderful years of marriage, building a life centered on love, family, and unwavering devotion. They raised two sons, Craig and Brian, and created countless cherished memories together.

A man with an unquenchable thirst for knowledge, Martin believed that learning was a lifelong pursuit. He earned two Bachelor of Science degrees, one in Agriculture and another in Mechanical Engineering—before going on to receive his Master's degree in Agricultural Engineering. His education laid the foundation for a distinguished career dedicated to helping others.

Martin began his professional career teaching Agricultural Engineering, first at UW Platteville, Wisconsin, and then SUNY Cobleskill, NY. He then spent more than 20 years as a Safety Consultant with Wausau Insurance, where his expertise and commitment to workplace safety earned him the respect of colleagues and clients alike.

Service to others was an important part of Martin's life. He was a proud member of the Shriners and, for more than a decade, volunteered countless hours transporting patients to Minneapolis, Minnesota, so they could receive the medical care they needed. He was also a dedicated Mason and an active member of the Merrill Historical Society. During the past five years, he found great joy in researching local history for the society, spending many hours uncovering and preserving stories from the past.

Martin enjoyed working with his hands and could often be found fixing things around the house or tending to his yard. He took pride in solving problems and making things better, whether it was repairing something broken or helping someone in need.

Above all else, Martin was a devoted husband, father, and grandfather. Until the very end, his greatest concern was making sure Kathleen was cared for. He was a loving and supportive father to Craig and Brian, and he took immense pride in his grandchildren, celebrating each of their accomplishments with genuine joy.

Martin will be remembered for his quiet strength, generous heart, sharp mind, and steadfast dedication to his family and community. His life was one of purpose, kindness, and service, and he leaves behind a legacy that will continue to inspire all who knew and loved him.

He will be deeply missed and forever remembered. He is survived by his wife, Kathleen; sons, Craig (Joan), and Brian (Kerri); and grandchildren, Brandon, Karissa, and Jillian; as well as several nieces and nephews.

In addition to his parents, he was preceded in death by his mother and father-in-law, Millard and Louise Ihlenfeldt; as well as his sister, Janette Jenson.

In accordance with his wishes, no services will be held. May his memory be of comfort to all that knew him. Memorials in his memory may be directed to the UW Foundation, Excellence in Cardiovascular Medicine Fund.

records.name: Michael John Ringwelski
records.summary: Michael John Ringwelski, age 83 passed away on Jul 10, 2026.
records.body:
Michael “Mike” J. Ringwelski, 83, Wausau, passed away peacefully surrounded by his family on Friday, July 10, 2026 at Aspirus Wausau Hospital.

He was born March 13, 1943 in Wausau, son of the late Michael F. and Helen (Langhoff) Ringwelski.

Mike spent many enjoyable years hunting, fishing and making firewood at his cabin in Harshaw. He always knew where the secret fishing spots were. Mike was an avid Packers and Brewers fan and he made sure he got in on every pool.

The family would like to extend a special thank you to all his many friends at his two favorite local pubs, Tic Toc and Chatterbox, who were there to assist him in any way.

He will be missed by his many friends and family. Mike fought a good fight and is now forever in God’s care.

Survivors include five siblings, Richard “Ringo” (Delores) Ringwelski, Wausau, MaryAnn Schmidt, Schofield, Florian “Bud” (Barb) Ringwelski, Tomahawk, Floyd Ringwelski, Wausau and BettyJane “BJ” Kimps, Harshaw; one sister-in-law, Evelyn Ringwelski, Ringle; and many nieces, nephews, relatives and friends.

Besides his parents, he was preceded in death by two siblings, Nancy Wilde and Dennis Ringwelski; three brothers-in-law, David Schmidt, Dennis Kimps and Jerome “Pork” Wilde; and one sister-in-law, Nancy Ringwelski.

In accordance with Mike’s wishes, private burial will be held.

Online condolences may be expressed at www.petersonkraemer.com

records.name: Scott M. Bouchard
records.funeral_home: Ascend Funeral Home & Cremation Care of Weston
records.summary: Scott Michael Bouchard, 63, of Marathon City, passed away peacefully on June 26, 2026.
records.body:
Scott Michael Bouchard, 63, Marathon City, passed away peacefully on Friday, June 26, 2026, at Aspirus Wausau Hospital's Palliative Care Unit, surrounded by his sons, daughter-in-laws, and brother, Thomas.

He was born on October 9, 1962, at USAF Hospital Lackland AF Base in Texas to Velma Mae Bouchard and Donald Alvin Bouchard. He graduated from Newberry High School in May 1981. In 1995, he became a proud father to Dylan Bouchard and then again in 1999, to Brett Bouchard.

Scott worked at Marathon Cheese for 35 years. He retired in 2024. In his free time during retirement, he'd spend time with his grandchildren. He loved teaching his grandchildren how to play the drums and enjoyed listening to them rock out on the drum set. He was an avid lover of music, specifically the drums, which he played for most of his life. He played in numerous bands from high school, into his adult life. He was also an avid Green Bay Packers fan. He loved golf, which he spent much time playing with his friends. He played up until he physically couldn't anymore. Other interests include Baseball, Hockey, cooking, movies, The Simpsons, and most importantly, spending time with his kids, and grandkids, and family.

Scott is survived by his children, Dylan Bouchard (Sami) of Merrill, WI; Brett Bouchard (Blanca) of Milwaukee, WI; grandchildren, Ava, Nicholai, Bailey, and Owen; as well as his mother, Velma Bouchard, of Newberry, MI. He is further survived by his siblings, Patricia Simi of Sault Ste. Marie, MI, David Bouchard (Jennifer) Bixby of OK, Richard Bouchard (Diane) of Newberry, MI, Thomas Bouchard of Tulsa, OK; as well as many nieces and nephews. He is preceded in death by his father, Donald Bouchard.

A Celebration of Life will be held at on Sunday, July 26, 2026, at 1:00 P.M. at the American Legion Post 74, 7964 State Highway M123, Newberry, MI.

The family wishes to thank Scott's old coworkers at Marathon Cheese for the card and well wishes during his time in the hospital, as well as a special thank you to his friends that visited him in the hospital.

records.name: Sister Joelle Mauer
records.summary: Sister Joelle Mauer, age 94 passed away on Jul 10, 2026.
records.body:
Sister Joelle Mauer, a Sister of Mercy of the Holy Cross, 94, died July 10, 2026, at Bell Tower Residence in Merrill, Wisconsin.

Helen (Sister Joelle) was born November 11, 1931, in Poniatowski, Wisconsin, to Joseph and Helen (Braun) Mauer. She entered Holy Cross Convent in Merrill, Wisconsin, on October 15, 1949, and professed her vows on June 19, 1951. Sister Joelle was in her 75 of ministry at the time of her death.

She attended Our Lady of the Holy Cross High School, Merrill Wisconsin, graduating in 1948. She earned her Master of Arts in Church Music and Liturgy degree in 1978.

In 1951, Sister Joelle along with Sister Maria Theodosia Laufer began Our Lady of Peace Apostolate in Vacherie, Louisiana which ministered in seven rural parishes along the Mississippi River as a religion teacher, liturgist, organist and choir director. In 1968, she was invited by Bishop Robert Tracy to open an Office of Liturgy in the Baton Rouge Diocese. She ministered as the Executive Director from 1968-1975. Sister Joelle then returned to Vacherie, Louisiana and ministered at three different parishes.

From 1980-1984 she was Director of Vocations for the Holy Cross Sisters. She then became Director of Liturgy and Music at St. Ambrose Cathedral in Des Moines, Iowa from 1984- 1995. Sister Joelle was the Director of Mercy Court Apartments in Des Moines, Iowa for several years before returning to Merrill to be the Coordinator for the retired Holy Cross Sisters living at Bell Tower Residence. In 2001-2004 she moved to Bellaire, Michigan to be the organist and choir director at St. Luke Catholic Church.

In 2010-2015, she moved to Kentucky to minister to the poor by working in soup kitchens, the homeless shelter and the HELP office interviewer. She then returned to Merrill and volunteered as the pianist for liturgies at Bell Tower Residence.

She retired to Bell Tower Residence in 2020. With decreasing sight and hearing she needed to give up her great love of accompanying at liturgies.

Sister Joelle has also served the Holy Cross Sisters as a Provincial Councilor several times. Sister Joelle’s love for and gift of music was always a part of her ministry. She was an avid reader and loved to play cards.

Preceding her in death are her parents Joseph and Helen, her sisters, Evelyn, Marcella O’Malley, Bernice Slattery and brothers, Gilbert, Matthew and Gregory.

She is survived by her family of Holy Cross Sisters and associates, as well as many nieces and nephews, great nieces and great nephews.

A Funeral Mass for Sister will be held on July 28, at 10:00 am in Holy Cross Chapel in Bell Tower Residence. Father Charles Hoffmann will officiate. Burial will follow in the St Francis Catholic Cemetery, Merrill, WI.

Special thanks to chaplains John Bourke and Jen Hoffman and the staff of Bell Tower Residence along with Interim Healthcare hospice nurse and staff for assisting in the spiritual and health care of Sister Joelle. An extra thanks to Holy Cross Sister companions Jamely and Sherry.
